Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty unconventional. I'd block out chunks of time, maybe four or five hours at once, where I'd really dive deep into the material through hands-on practice and working through problems myself. I didn't follow a strict daily schedule because I found I learned better in intensive bursts rather than spreading things thin. Most of my actual learning happened away from the computer, working through concepts physically with notes and diagrams spread across my desk.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit, though not really because of deadlines. I'd study intensively when I felt mentally ready for it, which might mean three heavy days one week and then lighter engagement the next. I've learned that forcing myself to study when I'm not in the right headspace is just wasted time. The deadlines mattered less to me than finding those windows where I could really absorb the material kinesthetically.
Q3: Honestly, I logged in mainly to grab materials I needed or check if there was anything new posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to see the video content, though I'd often download what I could and work through it offline. It wasn't really a daily habit for me—more like periodic check-ins when I needed specific resources. I found the constant logging in and out kind of tedious when I could just work from my own notes.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine the way my physical study materials did. I'd use it in bursts—downloading videos, checking announcements—but then I'd disappear for days working through everything offline. I suppose around exam time I'd check in more frequently, but it never felt like a habit, more like a tool I accessed when necessary.
Q5: The video content was genuinely useful—I watched nearly 200 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and really work through concepts at my own pace. I'd often watch a section, then pause and physically work through the problems myself before continuing. The peer interaction features were also helpful when I actually used them; bouncing ideas off other students helped solidify my understanding. Anything I could engage with actively rather than passively reading was valuable to me.
Q6: I largely ignored the discussion forums beyond minimal participation. Typing out responses felt disconnected from how I actually learn—I process things better through doing than through writing about doing. I also didn't complete many of the smaller assignments because they felt like busywork when I was already mastering the material through my own methods. If something didn't contribute directly to my understanding, I probably skipped it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could physically engage with it or when it directly connected to exam preparation. If I could work through problems, manipulate concepts, or apply knowledge practically, I was all in. Reading lengthy text-based materials or participating in abstract discussions felt less valuable because they didn't match how my brain processes information. I needed to feel like I was building skills, not just consuming content.
Q8: Required activities sometimes felt like boxes to tick rather than genuine learning opportunities, which made me a bit resentful when they didn't align with my learning style. Optional activities I could take or leave based on whether they actually helped me understand the material better. Ironically, I sometimes found optional practice problems more valuable than required discussion posts because I could engage with them hands-on. The distinction mattered less than whether the activity actually contributed to my learning.
Q9: Absolutely, this was the majority of my studying. I'd download lecture materials and videos, then work through everything with physical notes, flashcards, and practice problems I'd write out by hand. I had textbooks and external resources spread across my desk constantly. My best learning happened when I was physically manipulating information—drawing diagrams, solving problems on paper, even pacing around my room talking through concepts aloud.
Q10: Most of my exam preparation looked like nothing in the LMS. I'd spend hours working through practice problems on paper, creating physical study aids, and testing myself without any digital footprint. When I was really in the zone, I might study for five or six hours straight with zero online activity. My 92% exam score came from intensive offline preparation that the system never saw.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once to get the gist, then download it or take detailed notes and never return to the LMS version. My notes became my primary resource, and I'd review and practice from those repeatedly. The initial access was just the starting point—the real engagement happened afterward in my own study space with my own materials.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I felt bored with the course, like the material wasn't challenging enough or the format wasn't engaging me properly. When that happened, I'd disengage from the LMS entirely and just focus on what I needed for exams. My attendance dropped because showing up to repetitive content felt like a waste of time when I could learn more efficiently on my own. The boredom was real, but I pushed through because I knew I could still perform well.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage, though not necessarily my learning. When life got busy, the LMS was the first thing I'd cut because I could still study effectively without it. Fatigue made me less likely to engage with online discussions or complete smaller assignments—I'd conserve my energy for the high-stakes studying. My task completion rate reflects this; I prioritized what actually mattered for my grade.
Q14: Sometimes I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing while my mind was elsewhere. I'd be logged in, technically watching content, but not really absorbing anything because I wasn't actively engaging with it. Those sessions felt hollow, and I'd usually have to redo the learning later through my hands-on methods. Passive consumption just doesn't work for me, even when I'm physically present on the platform.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments that felt like busywork or didn't align with my learning style. Writing discussion posts or completing worksheets that wouldn't help me on exams got pushed aside constantly. I knew they were part of my grade, but I calculated that my exam performance would compensate. Looking back, my assignment completion rate suffered, but I don't regret prioritizing deep learning over surface-level task completion.
Q16: Deadlines created brief spikes in my LMS activity, mostly for submitting whatever I'd completed. But they didn't really drive my studying—I prepared for exams on my own timeline regardless of intermediate deadlines. I'd sometimes cram assignment submissions right before due dates, which probably shows in my response times. The exam deadline was the only one that truly shaped my preparation intensity.
Q17: Feedback had mixed effects. Useful, specific feedback motivated me to adjust my approach and engage more deeply with certain topics. But generic feedback or grades without explanation felt pointless and didn't change my behavior. After my CBT session, I tried to maintain perspective about feedback, not letting it affect my mood too much either way. I focused on what I could learn from it practically.
Q18: The course structure was fairly flexible, which suited me because I could engage on my own terms. However, the heavy emphasis on online participation didn't match my kinesthetic learning style, so I naturally drifted toward my own methods. The exam weighting was high enough that I could succeed despite lower engagement with other components. A more hands-on structured approach would have kept me more consistently engaged with official materials.
Q19: The video content and downloadable resources made engagement easier because I could take them offline and work with them my way. What made it harder was the emphasis on discussion posts and online participation—these felt forced and didn't contribute to my actual learning. If there had been more interactive simulations or practical exercises built into the LMS, I probably would have used it more. The text-heavy, discussion-based elements just weren't designed for how I learn.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding without finding it—clicking around, rewatching videos, trying to grasp concepts but not succeeding in producing work. Others might be like me, where they're gathering materials to study offline, so their activity looks high but their submissions are low. There's also procrastination—being present on the platform without actually being productive. High activity doesn't equal effective learning.
Q21: That's basically my experience. My LMS activity was moderate at best, but I earned a Distinction because my real learning happened offline. I'd grab what I needed from the platform and then disappear to study in ways that actually worked for my brain. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing, and I think the system misses students like me who learn effectively through alternative methods.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often someone accesses materials, time spent on videos, and submission patterns. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and whether someone is actually learning versus just clicking through. They can't capture me working through problems at my desk for hours or discussing concepts with study partners in person. The logs show presence, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actively wrestling with the material until it makes sense and sticks. For me, that's hands-on—writing, drawing, practicing, testing myself. It's not about time spent logged in or discussions posted; it's about mental effort and genuine curiosity about mastering the content. Engagement is internal and active, not just behavioral compliance with course requirements.
Q24: The LMS would never show my hours of handwritten notes, the practice problems I created and solved, or the physical flashcards covering my walls during exam prep. It wouldn't capture my genuine understanding of the material or the satisfaction I felt when concepts finally clicked through hands-on practice. My boredom with certain formats and my strategic decisions about where to invest my energy—none of that appears in the data. The real story of my learning happened mostly offline.
